sentences.text: Yes-- no, the war was already on.
Just the Germans took over Czechoslovakia and [INAUDIBLE] took over Hungarian.
Is [INAUDIBLE] and I-- the next day I went home.
Yeah.
Where I was born.
Seliste, yeah.
That's right.
And they came-- gave us pass too.
No, no.
Just the next day I just went home, back home.
When I came home, I mean, everything was OK.
The parents was home.
Everything was OK.
And let's go on with life.
And that was in '43.
And one day, when they came, my sister was taken with her family.
In '42, 1942, she was taken.
And one morning they come and they said to, about 11 o'clock, be ready.
We are coming to take you.
And I never know where they wind up.
Just I was there, and I wanted to go with my sister.
And then they told me, you will have time to go.
I just didn't understand.
Yeah.
I was there.
I helped them to pack.
I helped-- she'd got smaller children.
I wanted to go on with her.
I didn't know where they're taking her.
And I said, I'm going alone.
And then some policeman-- a Hungarian-- said, you will have time enough to go on there where she's going.
And there they took her.
And that's why I never know where she disappeared with the children.
No.
No.
No.
Nothing.
Not a thing.
We never heard from them.
Nothing.
And then came in [? 4093 ?].
They came and they said the same thing to us, be ready.
In the middle of the night, we will take you all out in the house.
It was a miserable rainy day.
And I, with my brothers, we tried to run away, although they catch them on the road, and they take us back.
My brother said, now, let's jump in the water.
No.
We went home.
Everybody was--
No, no.
First with all of my brothers.
And when it's come-- the last-- when they're supposed to come and to take us from the house, I went to-- I asked my one brother.
I say, let's run away.
He said, no.
They will catch us, and they will kill us.
Then I was thinking, and I went to the room-- my father was sitting on the bed-- and I went in the room.
And I said, father, I'm not going.
I'm-- I'm going some place hiding, and I'm not going.
He said, my child, I cannot help you.
You cannot help me.
[CRYING] And my younger sister, she heard this.
She was maybe 9, 10 years old.
And she came, and she hugged-- started to kiss me, and she said, we will never see each other.
And then never think about this, they will kill us.
They said that we are-- we are not citizens.
And that's what I run away.
I was thinking, I will go in an office, and I will take paper.
I will show them we are citizens.
But they just told us this.
They tricked us, just.
And then I just ran out from the house a half-hour before they were supposed to come and to take them, the rest.
By myself, yeah.
The brother didn't want to go, and a younger from me.
He didn't want to go.
He said they will catch us, and they will kill us.
And they took the two-- three brothers, and they took two sisters.
Two sisters.
One of them was married, and they was already [INAUDIBLE].
And I ran away.
They took everybody.
I ran away.
Well, first, in the middle of the night, I was sitting not far from that house.
I wanted to see.
And when they were walking out from the house, I just hear my mother ask them-- she said, I baked bread.
I want to take-- I'm going  with my children.
I'm want to take bread on the road.
And they told her, don't worry about your children.
You don't need it, your bread.
Your children will get bread.
That's what they told her.
And after they passed me by, I just went in the forest, deeper.
It was hills there.
It was very bad, but I had to go through a deep water.
I could swim, and on the river, scared while it was rainy and windy and dark outside.
It was terrible.
But somehow, I went there.
But I wanted to go in the forest.
And I was sitting there in the night, in the day, and the next night.
And then on the third night, I was thinking, I have to go on back.
I know that-- that one lady, our neighbor, they didn't take her.
They didn't took they said, the old people.
Her husband was killed in the first war, and that's why they didn't took the lady.
She got two sons.
The sons was not home.
And when they hear what happened, they came back.
And on the third day, in the night-- the middle of the night-- I went, and I knocked on the door.
And she said, you should come in.
Before, well, she said they will not take any more.
They stopped.
They said that's it.
Well, they took the rest what was left.
They didn't left too much-- those who were not home, and the older-- the older ladies, or the men with us.
But like my grandma, they didn't touch her, and the lady there.
Then I was by the lady's house.
I didn't feel to go back in my house.
But then I went back in that house, it was almost like cleaned out, this house.
Cleaned out.
People took all the things.
My house.
Yeah.
And then in the middle of the night, when I ran away, I sent a postcard to my brother.
He was in Bratislava.
This was in the Slovaka-- in Slovakia, in the hopes that he was there in school.
And I sent him a postcard, and I told him I'm alone here.
They took away the whole family.
The next day, he came home.
When he came home, my brother, he started to ask me to leave-- leave everything.
He knew more like I-- he understood more like I did.
I didn't.
Even my little sister told me we will not see each other.
I was then about-- I don't know. 20-- 22, 21.
Yeah, 22.
Although I didn't believe that it can be this, to kill people.
And  my brother came home, and he asked me-- he cried for me.
Come, he said.
I have their people.
They will take care of us.
And I said, they will come back, and they will not have anywhere to go in.
This house-- somebody will take the house.
Somebody will take the rest of things from the house.
And he just didn't want it-- didn't want it, forced me to come.
Let's go on, and he just was staying with me.
And he was staying like a few weeks.
And I don't know, they came, and they said that he's a communist, my brother.
And they took him.
Although it's true, I saw him a few times to get together with men.
I didn't know who they are-- who they are and what they were.
I didn't know nothing about this.
And they came and they took him, and they said to him, say goodbye to your sister.
And what my-- what my brother told me, he said, if you survive, go to Palestine.
It was no Israel then.
He said, go to Palestine.
That's all.
That's all.
Then after, they keep them there, and I don't know what they did with him.
They came, and they told me, come on, you will say goodbye to your brother.
And I said, look.
I want to take something for him.
They said, he don't need nothing.
He took me there in a room.
He was there.
He looked terrible, and just to say goodbye to him.
And he told me again the same words-- please, if you survive, go to Palestine.
And he told me, you always will see your brothers, your sisters, at the same age.
They will be already old people.
You will-- and that's how was it.
All of us seemed young children.
[CRYING] And then I didn't hear more from him.
No, never.
Never.
No.
I tried.
I tried when I came-- I didn't go home when-- after the war.
I didn't go home.
No.
I didn't see my parents.
It was after the high holidays, in the night, they started to run and to take off and come in the Spitalska.
They took them there.
Yes.
Yes.
I know where they was taken.
And then they catch them on the road, they just took them back, and they killed them.
They put them in a-- where it was like horses, uh--
Stable.
And they put in petrol, in gas around, and they just burned them.
And that's how they died, all of them.
Yeah.
One man, with a daughter, run away.
This was a second cousin of my mother's.
And he was not more young.
He runned away from there.
He was together with them.
That's what he told me.
My one sister, she died, she was 16 years old.
Some soldiers, they raped her, and then they just put a knife in her stomach.
[CRYING] And my father was fighting with them.
They beat him up so he couldn't do nothing.
They left her so.
And that was the end of my parents.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then when they came to take the rest of Jews from the village, I got two offers, where they wanted to hide me out.
I didn't want it more.
I didn't want to hide anymore.
I didn't want it, running away.
I didn't want it, nothing.
They wanted to help me.
One was--
A girl, what she worked in office, she came, and a boy.
I used to go with them together in school.
They got a house in forest for the summer.
And he said, you can sit there.
Nobody's going to come in there.
Nobody will see you, and I will see that you have to eat.
And they knew that-- that they did take us to kill.
And he said, if you want it, I will take your things from the house.
I will see that you have everything what-- what you needed.
And I-- I didn't say even thank you.
Nothing.
I just was-- no.
I didn't want it, more hiding.
Maybe because of my grandma, I don't know why.
And then--
Well, my grandma still was alive, and she was still home.
She was close to her 80s.
Maybe she was already 80s.
Well, when they came to take her from the house, she was fighting with them.
They kicked her, the police, and everybody.
She was a fighter, my grandma.
Yeah.
And she was maybe 80 years.
She didn't want to leave from going out from the house.
Well, it didn't help.
And when they told-- they came-- I think was like after a Pesach-- they came, and they took us all.
And I just put on-- I got a brand new coat, got some money and something-- a few things-- and I just put my hand in pocket, and I walked out from the house.
And that's it.
They took us in ghetto.
They took all of in the city.
Yeah.
Nobody was there.
Just if somebody hides someplace.
But nobody was left more, not one Jew.
The old and the young one, everybody was gone.
And they took us in the ghetto.
It was first [INAUDIBLE]-- Chust, Chust.
We was there in the ghetto.
A city, yes.
The-- it was a city, this.
And they took us all there, and it was terrible there.
It was old people, young people, babies, with children.
It was terrible, what-- what was in the ghetto.
I was staying in one room with my grandmother, yeah.
And then the morning, we wait.
In the morning, then we can go out from the-- not from the ghetto, from the house, from the barrack what we was there.
Then we'd go out a little, stand in outside.
There was a few girls together, and then they came and they took us in the kitchen to cook for the people in the ghetto.
Then we cooked there, we worked there.
And then we took the food, brought in the ghetto, and we get for the-- to eat.
[SIGHS]
No, I was not cooking.
I was just help-- it was people what they cooked there.
We was just-- the girls, we just washed the dishes, peeled the potatoes.
Those things were what we did.
And then we was going to maybe a week in the kitchen there to work, and then after this, we would stand outside and wait till they come to take us.
He came, a German soldier from the Wehrmacht.
Not a SS man.
He was from the Wehrmacht.
And we were still there, and he would go with-- with his finger, he said, Come.
We didn't say-- we didn't ask where and what.
We didn't know where he was taking us.
It was about six or seven girls, I don't know.
Maybe just six.
And he didn't talk to us with the whole way.
We didn't-- we didn't talk between us, where is he taking us.
We didn't know.
He brought us there of a place where the SS was came, and he said we have to work here, to washing the laundry.
And it was not water.
It was like a second floor.
There was not water in the house.
He said, two of you will bring enough water down that you will wash, and they'll do this and this.
And here I'm standing, he's not giving me nothing what to do.
I say OK.
Then he came to me.
He said, in German, come with me.
He took me there, and was a hallway, an open-- open terrace it was, around this building.
And he took me in there in a room, and he said, you have to clean in here for [INAUDIBLE].
We have to make clean this room.
And he showed me how to make the bed, he showed me everything.
He was really a nice man.
He showed me everything.
Then he said, I have to go on out from here.
He said, well, the girl will take a think, and then we'll be making something better here.
He was really-- I don't-- never know his name.
He didn't know my name.
Nothing.
And this was maybe for two or the three weeks.
He was coming to take us in the morning, in the evening, he dropped us off.
And he always came to me.
He'd ask me if the SS would come in there.
He'll be beaten if they're doing this, and what they're doing when they come in.
It was-- I got never to tell him what they do.
And then he asked me to--
Well, they used to come in and beating and taking out men.
And ask-- ask them to dance and to sing.
And they used to beat them.
And they used to come home just with blood.
Everything was just with blood for the-- for the boys-- the men.
That's what they used to do.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then he asked me to ask some men if they wanted to smoke cigarettes, to take their money, and he will buy.
And he will give me the-- that I give them for the men, or the the medicine or the soap, what they needed.
He just asked me to ask them for money.
He was very-- all of us thanked him.
But he was not a German.
Maybe he was a Jew-- a German Jew.
He was not from the SS.
We was a-- from the Wehrmacht, with the soldiers, the regular army.
And one day after, I don't know, three or the four weeks, but he took us to work there.
As he come one day, and the girls went to work, and he said to me, you don't have to do today the room.
And I was thinking, I will just stand there [INAUDIBLE] with the girls.
He said, no.
You come with me, and we will go out in the city.
I have to buy something, and I cannot speak.
I don't understand.
You will be like a [NON-ENGLISH].
You will help me.
We went a little further from the house, and he-- he's telling me he knows that he will not come to take us tomorrow more.
He asked me, if I wanted, he will take me-- he will make for me German papers, that  I am a German girl.
And he said, you will go in in the army.
Well, a lot of German girls are in the army.
You don't know me.
I don't know you.
Well, I'm today here.
Tomorrow, I can be at another place, and the same with you.
If you wanted, then I can make you such a paper.
I said no.
I just didn't want it.
I-- I didn't want to live more.
And I told him no.
The next day, he didn't come more.
He was telling.
After I was thinking that's-- he knew it already.
And two, three days later, they took us of Auschwitz.
They put us in the-- the train like-- like cows there, no windows, no nothing.
Just a little window there.
We was a line of one of the other on we was making, the ones under yourself.
Didn't-- got nothing, not what to eat.
Nothing.
The little children cried.
It was-- you couldn't take this.
It was terrible.
So that's how dark the room was.
The-- he took the knife and he cut his throat there, between all the people.
Nobody screamed.
Nobody went even not to let him do this.
Everybody was just-- just like-- I don't know, like in the Twilight Zone.
We didn't know what is going on.
And then they brought us of Auschwitz.
They kept us there maybe a day, still in the-- in the train.
No.
In Auschwitz.
Oh, sure I was, but they-- he took us in the morning and brought us in the evening, afternoon.
Back-- back in the ghetto.
He brought us back every day, and there was my grandma.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when we came to Auschwitz, they kept in the trains.
They just opened the door, so that nobody went out.
We shouldn't go out from the train.
Then we saw one side was water, the other side was a fire burning.
And we didn't know which-- we were thinking-- first, we saw the water, then we were thinking they will throw us in the water.
Then we saw the fire, and we was thinking they will burn us there.
And then we were thinking maybe we should undress and, well, you know, we've got our coats-- something warm-- on.
Well, we were just sitting there and waiting, And just like-- like dead people.
Then they-- they come, and they start to knocking, out!
Out from there!
From the train.
We came, we went out, and they started to separate us then. and And my-- I was holding my grandma by the hand while she's-- you know, she was-- I think she was 80 years old, maybe even more.
But she was still-- And then I met a man there by the train.
He was with his striped suit on.
This was Jewish men's-- what they was in camp, too.
And he pushed her on one side and pushed me on the other side.
And she started to cry.
And she said, I've got 72 children, I mean, with grandchildren, with grand-grandchildren, and you're the one what's left, and you are leaving me alone here.
I think guard, they just kicked me and then pushed me, and that's it.
And they took them right in gas chambers, all the old people.
There was a mother with four or five boys with us on this.
They took her children and I think her husband, too, still-- straight in-- in gas chamber.
She survived.
Well, they-- they pushed her away from the children.
They took just the children.
She went with me, yeah.
She lives now in California.
I know her very good.
I know her before the war.
Come and they took us to a place, they cut off the hair to one hair.
Her ?] got one hair, they cut off.
It's-- I got long hair when I was there, and I always was-- still got two or three hairs.
I was imagining how long my hair was.
Without hair, they ask us undress.
We undressed.
Naked.
They took us in shower, naked.
It was going all like this.
I was-- and then from the shower, they-- they count us, how many, somebody didn't run away.
And I was standing like this while I was naked, and he come with a stick and beat me up on the back, the SS there.
Then I was standing already straight.
I couldn't stand straight.
Well, it was terrible, the-- the back.
Then they gave us-- for everybody-- a black dress.
And they put such a yellow stripes on it.
From the back, if we run away, then nobody would-- then we cannot run, and they will recognize us.
They didn't give nothing, underwear.
Nothing, just the one-- a dress.
So that's-- that's how we was.
OK.
That's finished already.
And that's how we was in-- in Auschwitz.
No.
It was [GERMAN].
That means-- that was what they will kill us all.
That's why they didn't give numbers.

The girls what they took right away to work, and when they took us to work, when I was going to work, when they took us to Boizenburg there in the factory, in the the airplane factory, they didn't give more the numbers.
